coredata.dc:title: Sustainable agronomic response of carbon quantum dots on Allium sativum: Translocation, physiological responses and alternations in chromosomal aberrations
coredata.dc:creator: Vijeata, Anjali
Chaudhary, Savita
Chaudhary, Ganga Ram
Umar, Ahmad
Baskoutas, Sotirios
coredata.dc:description: The revolutionary growth in the usage of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in different areas have ultimately directed their discharge in the environment and further augmented the exposure of agricultural crops to these released particles. Therefore, the aim of current study is to evaluate the uptake, translocation and phytotoxicity of blue emissive CQDs on Allium sativum plant. The genotoxicity and cytotoxicity assessment of CQDs towards Allium sativum roots was estimated as function of three different concentrations. Considering the role of CQDs in promoting seed germination at 50 ppm concentration, a greenhouse experiment was performed to evaluate their effect on plant growth. Systematic investigations have shown the translocation of CQDs and their physiological response in terms of increased shoot length wherein P-CQDs exhibited more accumulation into Allium sativum parts. Our investigations unfold the opportunity to utilize Aegle marmelos fruit derived CQDs as a growth regulator in variety of other food plants.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The revolutionary growth in the usage of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in different areas have ultimately directed their discharge in the environment and further augmented the exposure of agricultural crops to these released particles. Therefore, the aim of current study is to evaluate the uptake, translocation and phytotoxicity of blue emissive CQDs on Allium sativum plant. The genotoxicity and cytotoxicity assessment of CQDs towards Allium sativum roots was estimated as function of three different concentrations. Considering the role of CQDs in promoting seed germination at 50 ppm concentration, a greenhouse experiment was performed to evaluate their effect on plant growth. Systematic investigations have shown the translocation of CQDs and their physiological response in terms of increased shoot length wherein P-CQDs exhibited more accumulation into Allium sativum parts. Our investigations unfold the opportunity to utilize Aegle marmelos fruit derived CQDs as a growth regulator in variety of other food plants.

ce:section.ce:para: The progressive leap in nanotechnological sector is considered as one of the revolutionary developments in science (Ashraf et al., 2021; Alouane et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2020; Jassby et al., 2018). The potential usage of advanced nanomaterials has initiated colossal range of breakthroughs in industrial sector for the betterment of the world (Umar et al., 2021a, 2021b, 2022; Hu et al., 2022; Chauhan et al., 2022). The improvement in optical, magnetic, electrical, fluorescent, thermal and catalytic properties of nanoparticles has further enhanced their applications in optoelectronics, pharmaceuticals, and energy production sector (Rosaline et al., 2022; Umar et al., 2021c, 2021d, 2021e; Fang et al., 2021; Afonin et al., 2020; Xin et al., 2019; Rezk and Allam, 2019; Zhao et al., 2021). Recently, nanomaterials have been extensively used for addressing various challenges related to plants and their growth in agriculture sector (Lowry et al., 2019; Won et al., 2021; Farooq et al., 2021; Muhammad, 2021). The utilization of nanoparticles as antibacterial and antifungal agents has also been documented in literature (Schnaider et al., 2019; Cheng et al., 2021; Tippannanavar et al., 2020). The revolutionary progress of nanotechnology in textiles, paints, dyes, medicines, bio-imaging agents, chemo microwave therapy and cosmetics has further enhanced their extensive usage in industrial sector (Zhang et al., 2021; Abou Elmaaty et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2020; Pervez et al., 2020; Yalikun et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Agbaje et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021). Therefore, researchers have developed various types of organic, inorganic, metallic biocomposites and carbon-based nanoparticles for targeting their repercussions on ecological surroundings (Ouyang et al., 2022; Shah et al., 2021; Rajput et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2020; Kumari et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2022).

In this framework, carbon-based nanoparticles, particularly zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots [CQDs] have drawn specific propensity due to their unique physicochemical properties (Liang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2022). Out of different types of carbon-based nanoparticles, CQDs have been extensively utilized in various types of biological, optical and catalytic applications (Zhang et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2021; Tang, 2021; Jadhav et al., 2021). The highly biocompatible nature with superior photostability and controllable emission properties has further enriched CQDs as one of the effective materials in biosensing, drug delivery, gene delivery, bioimaging, lithium ion batteries, optoelectronic devices and catalysis (Ma et al., 2020a; Milenkovi ć et al., 2021a; Jiang et al., 2022; Jing et al., 2022; Rohilla et al., 2021; Liu and Mao, 2021; Arun et al., 2021). Up to now, diverse range of synthetic methodologies such as solvothermal, hydrothermal, arc discharge, microwave and laser ablation methods have been used for the fabrication of CQDs (Zhao et al., 2019; Raveendran et al., 2019; Kung et al., 2020). In addition, biological means have also been used for the large-scale production of CQDs (Komalavalli et al., 2020). Many analysts have used economically viable and eco-friendly precursors including seeds, vegetable, leaves, fruits, rice or wheat husk, grass for the synthesis of CQDs and utilized them in diverse range of applications (Xu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2020; Boobalan et al., 2020; Zhang and Luo, 2021; Vijeata et al., 2021). The augmented usage of CQDs in different fields has ultimately directed their discharge in the environment and further enhanced the exposure of agricultural crops to these released particles (Li et al., 2020). Consequently, CQDs has become one of the supreme materials for investigating their physiological responses on plants. Recent investigations have revealed the potential of CQDs as growth regulators for plants and supported their photosynthetic activities. In addition, CQDs can also been useful for the enhanced synthesis of chlorophyll content, carotenoids and assimilation of CO2 in plants(Li et al., 2021)) However, there is apparent lack of research activities on consequential cytotoxic/mutagenic effects of CQDs on cellular biology of tested plants (Chauhan et al., 2020). For instance, Zhang et al. have synthesized chiral CQDs through hydrothermal treatment and demonstrated their effects on the growth of mung bean plant (Zhang et al., 2018). Li et al. have fabricated five different types of CQDs and studied their beneficial effect on the growth and disease resistance behavior towards rice plant (Li et al., 2018). Moreover, our study involves the exploration of effects of CQDs on the nutrient quality of the soil which has not been examined earlier. The CQDs have the ability to cross the cell barriers and results in noticeable changes at the genetic levels. However, the dearth of studies in the existing literature to check the corresponding effect of concentration, precursor source and size of biomass derived CQDs to provoke physiological changes has initiated our interest to explore their effect on plants. Additionally, the translocation, physiological responses and alternations in chromosomal aberrations in presence of CDQs have been discussed in detail for proposing the precise mechanism of their uptake and transportation.

To check the hypotheses proposed, we designed and prepared three different types of CQDs using Aegle marmelos fruit parts. The different fruit parts offer characteristic physical and chemical properties in improving photosynthetic activities and fluorescence parameters during plant growth. In addition, the beneficiary effect of Aegle marmelos fruit derived CQDs in biological applications has not been reported in literature. Subsequently, the vegetable Allium sativum or garlic has been chosen as a representative plant species to investigate the corresponding effect of CQDs on plant growth and photosynthesis. Allium sativum belongs to Liliaceae family and holds considerable utilization in the traditional medicines (Anu et al., 2017). Allium sativum is commonly used as flavouring agents during food preparation (Cavagnaro et al., 2007). The other health blooming applications of Allium sativum include antipyretic, antithrombotic, anti-microbial, anti-diabetic and anti-atherosclerotic activities (Shao et al., 2020). The literature inspection discloses that the systematic analysis over the toxicological response of CQDs has not been confirmed by using the Allium sativum bioassay. The current report is one of the primary studies to review the dose-based impact of three different types of CQDs prepared from Aegle marmelos fruit on Allium sativum root tip cells. It provides insight into the factors which are directly linked to seed growth, root/shoot elongation and enhancement in chlorophyll/carotenoid content. This novel approach provides an easy and economically viable bio-assay for checking alternations in chromosomal aberrations with augmented concentration of CQDs. The respective effect of chosen CQDs on the cellular toxicity has been checked by using optical light microscopy, whereas genotoxic effects of CQDs have been checked by monitoring the mitosis stages and chromosomal aberrations in the root tip cells of Allium sativum. In addition, the optimal concentration (50 ppm) of CQDs has further been used to analyse their effect on plant growth and photosynthetic activity of Allium sativum. A series of experiments revealed that the as prepared CQDs can improve the root as well as shoot growth at 50 ppm concentration. The current work has also explained the translocation of CQDs and their physiological response in terms of shoot length. The internalization of CQDs has been further confirmed by confocal laser scanning microscopy and fluorescence emission spectral studies. The chlorophyll content measurement by using fluorescence technique has been turned out to be quite informative approach in this study and useful in differentiating light responses of chosen CQDs during photosynthesis. The present study concludes that the prepared CQDs imparts growth enhancement effect up to 50 ppm concentration while the augmented concentration might induce the genotoxic behavior at cellular level which leads to diminished growth of plant. The comparative role of chosen CQDs on Allium sativum plant has further overcome the existing knowledge gap about the effect of precursor sources on the toxicological response of CQDs. This approach will definitely open new avenues for the applicability of CQDs in stimulating plant growth where these CQDs could effectively be used as fertilizers for agricultural practices in order to meet the challenges related to plants and environment.

Hydrogen chloride (HCl) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were procured from Fisher Scientific with 99.9% purity. Methylene blue dye was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich with 98% purity. K054 soil testing kit was purchased from Himedia to scrutinize the quality of the soil. All the experiments were performed in deionised water.

Aegle marmelos fruit was acquired from botanical garden of Panjab University, Chandigarh, India, for the synthesis of CQDs. Allium sativum was purchased from local market of sector 15, Chandigarh, India.

The freshly ripened Aegle marmelos fruit has been chosen as an eco-friendly precursor for the synthesis of CQDs. Being, one of the commonly found plants in India, Aegle marmelos has possessed higher antiamoebic and hypoglycaemic activities and has acquired greater potential in Indian Ayurveda (Rajaram et al., 2018). In this regard, the synthesis of CQDs from different parts of Aegle marmelos fruit presents a novel fabrication approach. Subsequently, the current methodology followed the greener approach for synthesizing CQDs by applying fruit exocarp, pulp and mixture of pulp and gum of Aegle marmelos; resulting particles were termed C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs respectively. For the synthesis, freshly ripened Aegle marmelos fruit was thoroughly washed with deionised water and its thick outer layer, pulp and gum immersed in between layers of pulp has been gently separated out. Around, 10 g of each fruit part i.e. fruit exocarp, pulp and mixture of pulp and gum were weighed and dispersed separately in a beaker containing water. The resultant mixtures were vigorously stirred at 3000 rpm on a magnetic stirrer until a homogenous mixture was obtained. Afterwards, the obtained solutions were transferred into Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 8 h by using a muffle furnace (Khan et al., 2019; Jing et al., 2019). The reaction went through gradual loss of water molecules and carbonization process occurs. The resultant dark brown suspensions indicated the generation of CQDs and were then subjected to centrifugation at 3000 rpm (Fig. 1 a). The supernatant of the solution was then passed through 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialysed to produce pure CQDs. The as prepared CQDs solutions were then washed thrice with ethanol to remove further impurities followed by drying in oven at 60 °C. The solid particles thus obtained were used for further characterization and experimentation.

The detailed physicochemical characterization of as synthesized C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs were performed by using standard physical techniques including UV–Vis. spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The optical properties of CQDs were measured using LABINDIA UV–Visible (UV–Vis.) spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800 nm via quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length. The photoluminescence study of CQDs was carried out on PerkinElmer Spectrophotometer (LS 55 PL). The zeta potential measurements of CQDs were done using Delsa™ Nano C, Beckman Coulter at 25 °C. The morphological characterization and the sizes of CQDs were checked by FE-SEM (JEOLJSM 7500F) and HR-TEM (Hitachi H-7500 electron microscope) at 100 kV. The hydrothermal treatment of Aegle marmelos fruit was carried out by using AICIL muffle furnace and stainless-steel autoclave. The heating and stirring process was carried on IKA C-MAG HS7 stirrer. The centrifugation of the aqueous solution of CQDs was done on the REMI R-24 centrifuge. The chromosomal aberration study of Allium sativum roots was done with Nikon ECLIPSE E100 light microscope with an attached camera on the top of microscope. The imaging of CQDs inside different parts of plants was performed on Nikon ECLIPSE Ti2 confocal laser scanning microscope under the wavelength of 350 nm. XPS (Physical Electronics, Model – PHI 5000 Versa Probe III) has been performed to investigate surface state of prepared CQDs. The structural composition of CQDs was ascertained using PerkinElmer (RXI) FTIR spectrometer instrument in the wave range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The hydrodynamic size and polydispersity index of prepared CQDs was determined through MALVERN ZEN 1690 dynamic light scattering instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential measurements of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The characteristic electrokinetic potential of the CQDs were analysed with zeta potential measurements. This technique provides insight about the density of surface-active functional groups present over the surface of carbon quantum dots. The value of zeta potential determines the potential of CQDs to interact with negative charge of roots of Allium sativum.

The genotoxic analysis has been carried out by using the three different types of CQDs viz. C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs prepared from the different parts of Aegle marmelos fruit. Around thirty fresh and healthy cloves of Allium sativum were used for carrying out the genotoxicity assay. For the analysis, the old roots emerged out from the cloves of Allium sativum were separated and scratched out from the surface. A stock solution of finely crushed and dried CQDs with 250 ppm concentration was made and were further diluted to obtain the lower concentrations i.e. 50 ppm and 100 ppm. The three equal sized cloves have further been treated with different concentrations (50 ppm, 100 ppm and 250 ppm) of CQDs for 72 h and were kept in the dark conditions at room temperature. While the distilled water served as control for the analysis. The water level has been restored by adding extra droplets of water on daily basis to prevent the roots from drying during treatment process. Three replicates have been made for each concentration for statistical confirmation of the given observations.

The treated root tips were separated out from Allium sativum clove with the help of sterilized scissors after 72 h of interactional treatment with different concentrations of C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs. Afterwards, the washed root tips have been hydrolysed in 1M HCl for 20 min at 60 °C followed by 2 min of staining with 1% methylene blue dye. 1–2 mm of the stained root tip has further been cut out and placed over a glass slide. The cover slip has been used for covering the root tip followed by pressing the tip firmly with the help of thumb pressure. The micro slides prepared for each chosen CQDs and control have further been visualized synchronously under high-power light microscope at 100 × resolution. The distinct cell stages like prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase, interphase along with various chromosomal aberrations were counted using light microscope or through camera fixed at the top of the microscope.

The mitotic index, relative mitotic index, chromosomal aberration and phase index (Chaudhary et al., 2016; Sabeen et al., 2020) was calculated using following equations:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope imaging of treated Allium sativum cloves
ce:section.ce:para: Allium sativum cloves were thoroughly washed with water and surface sterilized to remove any kind of external impurities present over their surface. The washed cloves were placed over 5 ml beaker containing different types of CQDs solutions and were then kept under dark conditions at 30 °C. After three days, the cloves were subjected to light conditions. The seven days old treated cloves were then taken out and cleaned with deionised water. The thin section of treated roots, cloves and leaves were section cut into transverse/longitudinal ways and analysed under the confocal laser scanning microscope. The live imaging of CQDs inside the leaves of Allium sativum plant was further checked as a function of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field cultivation studies of treated Allium sativum cloves
ce:section.ce:para: The field cultivation studies of Allium sativum cloves treated with 50 ppm concentration of CQDs viz. C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs has further been carried out by hydroponic method (De Souza et al., 2021). Initially, Allium sativum cloves were surface sterilized with 3:1 of ethanol and water respectively, followed by washing with deionised water. The cloves were then placed over 5 ml beaker containing aqueous solution of CQDs under dark conditions with temperature maintained at 30 °C for 72 h. After 3 days, the treated cloves were exposed to light conditions with day/night temperature regime of 25 °C:20 °C respectively. For the plantation, each pot was packed with sieved and air-dried soil with 5 cm of upper surface left unpacked with soil for proper irrigation process. The seven-day old cloves of each treatment of control, C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs were then transplanted in pots at a distance of 5 cm apart.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of chlorophyll and carotenoid content in treated Allium sativum plant
ce:section.ce:para:
The chlorophyll and carotenoids content of green leaves emerged out from the treated Allium sativum plant was further analysed by applying spectrophotometric method in acetone dispersed leaf extract. The calculations were made according to AOAC Official Methods of Analysis (1990) and Lichtenthaler and Welburn (1983) (Almendros et al., 2015; Lichtenthaler et al., 1987). To avoid the error due to evaporation of volatile organic solvents, the absorbance measurements were done with cuvette having air tight lid. For the measurements, 0.5 g of each CQDs treated fresh leaf taken from the 45 days old plants were grinded using a mortar pestle and then dispersed in acetone. The obtained residue was then subjected to ultracentrifugation and the supernatant was utilized for analysis (Gao et al., 2021). The absorbance measurements were done in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The experiment was repeated thrice to ensure the accuracy. The total chlorophyll and carotenoids content were determined by spectrophotometric analysis by using the following equation:

where Ca, Cb, refers to chlorophyll (a) and chlorophyll (b) content (mg/L) and Ct is the sum of chlorophylla and chlorophyllb, A642.5 and A660 are the absorbance values at 642.5 nm and 660 nm respectively. The photoluminescence properties of chlorophyll were further scrutinized with the PL emission spectra and UV light exposure studies. The experiment was repeated thrice to ensure the reliability of the measurement.

To analyse the effect of employed CQDs on the quality of soil, a series of soil testing experiments were performed after 45 days of plantation study. Initially, the pH of the soil was determined. For experimentation, 5 ml of distilled water was poured in a clean test tube and 2 g of given soil sample was added to it. 0.5 g of reagent pH/R1 was added to the mixture, followed by shaking for 20 min. Afterwards, 5 drops of reagent pH/R2 was poured inside the solution and shaken well for 30 s. The test tube was allowed to stand for sometimes till the soil settles down and the colour of the supernatant was observed. The obtained colour was further matched with pH colour chart for checking the acidic nature of treated soil. The amount of oxidizable organic carbon has further been tested in the treated soil. For the analysis, 2 g of treated soil and 0.5 g of reagent OC/R1 were taken in a clean test jar. Around 10 ml of distilled water and 0.5 g of reagent OC/R2 were then added to the above mixture. The formed solution was then added with 2 ml of reagent OC/R3. The content of the test jar was shaken intermittently for 5 min followed by non-disturbed standing for 10 min. The test strips from soil testing kit was then dipped in supernatant and obtained colour was matched for oxidizable organic carbon. The phosphate, nitrate and potassium content of the treated soil samples were also tested for analyzing the effect of employed CQDs on the composition of soil (Kg per hectare). The detailed explanation of each experiment has been provided in supplementary data (Section S1). All the experiments were repeated thrice and the average results were obtained for each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of growth and fruit (cloves) yield of Allium sativum plant
ce:section.ce:para: The growth of Allium sativum was further assessed by monitoring the total root and shoot length for 7- and 45-days old plant with exposure of C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs. The water is chosen as control during the analysis. The measurement of the shoot length was taken from apex of the cloves to the tip of leaves. The harvested fruit was further weighed for each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural and optical characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Aegle marmelos is a slow ripening fruit and takes almost one year to attain its fully ripened stage. It consists of rigid-woody shell, pulp, flattened-oblong seeds and a sticky transparent mucilage immersed in between the seeds. The physico-chemical characteristics of Aegle marmelos varies in different fruit parts. Therefore, a facile hydrothermal method has been demonstrated for the fabrication of three types of CQDs using different components of fruit. The formed CQDs were initially confirmed through UV–vis. absorption measurement in the reflectance mode between 200 and 800 nm (Figure S1a). The bright blue emissive CQDs manifests two absorption bands (Figure S1a). The first absorption band at 223 nm, 235 nm, 237 nm for C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs were typically assigned to n-π* transition of CO bond and the second peak at 323 nm, 342 nm, and 360 nm were ascribed to π-π* transition of sp (Alouane et al., 2021) domains in formed particles. The obtained spectrum was much connected with an earlier reported data (Ma et al., 2020b). On comparing the absorption bands of three distinct types of CQDs, it has been found that M-CQDs have displayed red shift of 37 nm and 18 nm as compared to C-CQDs and P-CQDs respectively. This shifting in peak was mainly explained due to the formation of bigger sized particles in M-CQDs as compared to other two counterparts. The distinct interval of time analysis was also performed on prepared CQDs using optical absorption studies. From the results, the stable nature of synthesized CQDs was visualized with no shifting of the band position.

Additionally, the photo luminescent properties of CQDs were investigated using emission spectroscopy (Figure S1b). The emission peaks were observed at 430 nm with λ exc at 330 nm for C-CQDs and P-CQDs respectively. However, the emission maxima for M-CQDs was observed at 440 nm with λ exc at 330 nm. Supporting information Figure S1b shows the comparative emission peaks of C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs at different excitation wavelength. This is due to the distinct size of the particles along with the different surface chemistry associated with each surface trap of CQDs. The lack of other emission peaks in prepared CQDs have further depicted that the formed particles have not possessed any kind of surface defects. Furthermore, the surface charge of C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs determined through zeta potential measurements was found to be 0.25, 0.28, 0.12 mV (Figure S4a). The results determine the comparatively higher stability of C-CQDs and P-CQDs in contrast to M-CQDs.

The morphological and structural characterization of three different types of CQDs prepared from the fruit exocarp, pulp and mixture of pulp and gum of Aegle marmelos has been studied by using the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis. Fig. 1 (bi-biii) exposes the HRTEM images of prepared CQDs with high magnification. The images have not displayed any kind of crystallinity in the prepared particles and hence suggested the amorphous nature of all the three types of CQDs prepared by using the different fruit parts of Aegle marmelos. Furthermore, the particle size distribution was relatively uniform and narrow without any apparent agglomeration in each type of prepared CQDs. The obtained size of C-CQDs and P-CQDs was found to be comparatively smaller (5 nm) in contrast to M-CQDs (5–10 nm). Further the hydrodynamic radii of the particles were also determined through DLS measurements. The obtained particles i.e. C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs showed an average size of 43.65, 28.31, 50.83 nm along with the PDI value of 0.487, 0.495 and 0.501 respectively (Figure S2ai-aiii). Similarly, in FE-SEM micrographs (Fig. 1ci-ciii), the synthesized CQDs have acquired spherical and circular shape with a size distribution between 10 and 20 nm. The overlapping and agglomeration of some of prepared CQDs aroused due to the higher surface energy associated with the formed CQDs. Further, the elemental composition of CQDs was tested with energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) mapping (Fig. 1 cia to cic for C-CQD, ciia to ciic for P-CQDs and ciiia to ciiic for M-CQDs). With this, the red-coloured spot represents the carbon(C) content, whereas the yellow and green coloured spot pointed to the oxygen(O) and nitrogen(N) content respectively. These mapping images have clearly shown the existence of higher amount of carbon and oxygen in prepared CQDs as compared to nitrogen atom.

To experimentally determine the successful formation of CQDs, their structural composition has been determined through FTIR measurements. The FTIR spectrum shows the typical absorption peaks in all the three types of CQDs (Figure S2b). The spectrum of C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs shows a sharp absorption peak at approximately 3420 cm−1 that could be ascribed to –OH stretching vibration along with a shoulder at 3245 for -N-H stretching vibration which was absent in case of M-CQDs. A minor peak at 2930 cm−1 appeared in case of P-CQDs which was ascribed for C–H stretching vibration. In addition, the spectra of all the three CQDs showed an edged (in C-CQDs/P-CQDs) and flattened (in M-CQDs) peak at 1619 cm−1 which corroborates to CO vibration mode. The characteristic peak at 1108 cm−1 was assigned to C–O stretching vibrations wherein the peaks were diminished in case of C-CQDs and M-CQDs. The spectra of P-CQDs shows an additional peak at 1400 cm−1 corresponding to CC stretching vibration. Moreover, an intense vibrational band at 618 cm−1 exhibits the presence of = C–H stretching vibration in C-CQDs and P-CQDs, thus completely confirming the successful formation of CQDs (Figure S2b). Furthermore, the XPS spectra was obtained to investigate the surface state of the CQDs. The survey spectrum for all the three types of CQDs exhibits the existence of three major peaks for carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N). The carbon and oxygen weight content were much higher than the nitrogen. In addition, no other prominent peak was observed for any residual element which accentuates towards the purity of CQDs. The deconvoluted spectra of C1S in C-CQDs shows the dominant peak at 284.7 which was ascribed to the graphitic CC bond or the C–C bond. The minor peaks at 286.25 and 288.30 eV were typically assigned to the CO and O–CO bonds respectively Figure S3(aii). Devolution of O1S spectra in C-CQDs gives two main peaks at 531.58 and 533.10 eV which represent O1S states in the CO and C–O bonds respectively Figure S3(aiii). Further the high-resolution spectrum of N1S has been provided in Figure S3(aiv), shows peak at 399.5 eV which arises from the functional groups (-NH2) immobilised over the CQDs surface, thereby giving rise to C–N bond formation. The full scan XPS spectra of P-CQDs shows the existence of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. Notably, the deconvoluted spectra of C1S shows similar peaks at 284.7, 286.30, 288.14 eV for CC/C–C bond, CO bond and O–CO bond respectively with comparatively different intensities, height and area under the peak in contrast to other CQDs Figure S3(bii). The spectrum of O1S shown in Figure S3(biii) represents peaks at 531.24 and 532.67 eV corresponding to CO and C–O bonds respectively. Further the spectrum of N1S shows a major peak at 399.41 along with a minor peak at 402.51 eV which could be assigned to C–N and N–O bonds respectively Figure S3(biv). These results signify high degree of functionalization and further confirms the more stability associated with particles of P-CQDs. The survey scan of M-CQDs also exhibits three clear peaks at 284.2, 401.4, 501.1 eV which were ascribed to carbon, nitrogen and oxygen respectively Figure S3(ci). The high-resolution spectrum of C1S shows peaks at 284.7, 286.35 and 288.24 eV which indicated the existence of CC/C–C bond, CO bond and O–CO bond respectively Figure S3(cii). Furthermore, the deconvolution of O1S represents two peaks at 531.11 and 532.45 eV for CO and C–O bonds respectively with comparatively lower intensities and area in comparison to the particles derived from pulp and covering of Aegle marmelos Figure S3(ciii). As given in Figure S3(civ), the N1S spectra of M-CQDs shows two peaks at 399.72 and 400 eV with respect to C–N and N–C3 bonds. These outcomes were in consonance with results obtained from FTIR and elemental mapping and thus demonstrate the presence of various functional groups like –OH, –NH2, COOH over the surface of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of varied concentration of CQDs exposure on Allium sativum
ce:section.ce:para: In order to visualize the corresponding effect of different types of CQDs i.e. C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs on the rooting ability of the Allium sativum clove, a series of experiments have been carried out by immersing the bottom of the cloves in the aqueous solution of known concentration of CQDs. The roots emerged out from Allium sativum have not produced any kind of blackening effect in the presence of CQDs for all the employed concentrations. The length of the emergent roots in each chosen concentration was found to be around 1–2 cm long. However, on increasing the concentration of CQDs in aqueous media has produced an inhibitory effect over the number of emergent roots. For instance, the 50 ppm concentration of C-CQDs have produced around 16 roots, however, 18 and 12 roots were produced in presence of 50 ppm concentration of P-CQDs and M-CQDs. On doubling the amount of chosen CQDs (100 ppm) in reaction medium has decreased the number of rootlets to 9, 13, and 6 for C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs respectively (Fig. 2 b). However, the 250 ppm concentration of chosen CQDs has further reduced the number of roots to 8, 10 and 4 in Allium sativum cloves with respect to C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs. The comparatively highest number of roots in P-CQDs (even at higher concentration) might have resulted from augmented interactions between negatively charged surface of roots and the positively charged molecules of P-CQDs in contrast to C-CQDs and M-CQDs. In addition, the roots exposed with CQDs were found to be little thicker as compared to control (Fig. 2a) which is due to the occurrence of electrostatic interactions in CQDs treated cloves with reference to control.

Additionally, the one-week exposure of clove with 50 ppm concentration of all types of chosen CQDs have also shown the emergence of leaflets at the tip of Allium sativum cloves. The results have supported the non-genotoxic behavior of CQDs at 50 ppm concentration and were more preferably utilized for further studies. Moreover, a small shoot was observed in presence of 100 ppm and 250 ppm concentration of P-CQDs as compared to C-CQDs and M-CQDs. The results have further supported that the CQDs prepared from the pulp (P-CQDs) of Aegle marmelos fruits has produced growth promoting effect on Allium sativum as compared to particles produced from outer covering (C-CQDs) and mixture of gum and pulp (M-CQDs). The corresponding values of shoot length at 50 ppm concentration of CQDs after 45 days of treatment were shown in Fig. 2c. This study indicates the non-poisonous and the growth promoting behavior of P-CQDs which will further enhance their scope in agricultural fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internalization of CQDs in Allium sativum
ce:section.ce:para: We have further investigated the internalization of CQDs in the Allium sativum plant by means of fluorescence spectroscopy (Figure S5). The variations in the fluorescence intensity are summarized together with the photomicrographs of the treated cloves (Fig. 3 ). Here, the highly carbonaceous composition of Aegle marmelos derived CQDs has proved EDAX as an inappropriate source to analyse the incorporation of CQDs in Allium sativum plant. Therefore, internalization studies of CQDs after 7 days’ exposure in rootlets were determined through PL emission spectra by taking original CQDs solution as control. For each measurement, 500 μL solution of chosen CQDs from corresponding beaker with incubated Allium sativum cloves was diluted with water and subjected for fluorescence measurement (Figure S5). It has been found that the photoluminescence intensity of each type of chosen CQDs is decreased with the passage of time. However, the fluorescence intensities of original CQDs solution placed under similar conditions exhibits negligible changes in the emission intensity. This reduction in the PL intensity in presence of Allium sativum cloves has been explained due to the internalization of particles in roots. The fluorescence intensity has shown linear decrease with subsequent days of treatment. However, there was maximum reduction in fluorescence intensity on 7th day of treatment with CQDs due to the maximum incorporation of CQDs inside the roots. Further, the internalization of CQDs in Allium sativum rootlets have been observed under confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) with 350 nm laser irradiation. The seven days old cloves incubated with 50 ppm concentration of C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs has been taken for the analysis. The results were compared with control for validating the translocation of CQDs in different parts of plants (Fig. 3). On interpreting the results, it has been found that the CQDs treated cloves has shown the existence of strong in vivo bluish coloured auto fluorescence under confocal laser microscope.

The results supported the photoluminescence data and confirmed the easily movement of CQDs across the intricate networks of extracellular matrix formed by pectin, cellulose and hemicellulose in cell wall of Allium sativum plants. However, the confocal images of control group have not displayed any significant variations in photoluminescence of external particles nor the auto fluorescence of biological tissues (Fig. 3). This can be compared with recently reported data by Qian et al. who has showed that the pumpkin seeds when exposed to surface functionalised carbon dots indicates accumulation inside pumpkin roots and shoots (Qian et al., 2018). From the bright field confocal images of Allium sativum clove, it has been inferred that the CQDs were primarily accumulated inside the clove through intercellular spaces present in its outermost layer. After the emergence of roots, the CQDs uptake takes place through the surface of rootlets via electrostatic interactions. The presence of surface functional moieties over CQDs and zeta potential results has further supported the electrostatic interactions between the positively charged CQDs with the negatively charged surface of roots tips, thereby resulting in absorption of CQDs. The more penetration of particles in case of P-CQDs treated cloves results from the positively charged surface of P-CQDs which further enhanced their uptake efficiency by rootlets. Furthermore, the absorbed CQDs were translocated from roots to fruit, stems and leaves along with water through vascular system (Chen et al., 2020). The longitudinal section of stem has further reflected the luminescence of CQDs around vascular bundles which in turn consist of discrete strands of xylem and phloem (Fig. 3). The vascular bundles of stem were continuous with vascular system of leaves and helps in translocation of the absorbed CQDs into the extracellular spaces around the leaf margins via apoplastic pathway. Furthermore, it was worth mentioning over here that P-CQDs particles have exhibited higher uptake and translocation in different parts of Allium sativum plant in contrast to C-CQDs and M-CQDs (Fig. 3). However, M-CQDs treated cloves have not reflected any remarkable influence over the roots, shoot and fruit of Allium sativum.

In contrast to higher dimensional nanomaterials, 0D CQDs possess spherical or quasi spherical symmetry with an average diameter lesser than 10 nm. Due to the excellent size distribution and spherical symmetry, the particles exhibited higher optical density, wavelength dependent luminescence emission as well as enhanced cellular permeability. By the virtue of these novel properties, P-CQDs manifests excellent dispersibility, greater translocation in cells and enhanced fluorescence response during confocal imaging due to their smaller size in contrast to other CQDs. Upon the effective passivation inside the plant cells, these properties get greatly enhanced and further induce changes at chromosomal level. Therefore, from the results it can be inferred that the enhanced in vivo bioluminescence in case of P-CQDs treated cloves are due to comparatively higher surface charge and smaller particulate size of formed particles, which further assisted their higher rate of internalization in the cell and more dispersal around the distinct cell organelles. In addition, the highly soluble nature of prepared particles has further assisted their movement via xylem and phloem inside the plant cell. However, the high agglomeration tendency of M-CQDs gave rise to large sized clusters in aqueous media which further clogs the root surface and results in lesser uptake of particles. The corresponding effect of different time intervals have been carried out to further comprehend the absorption and translocation potential of prepared CQDs in leaflets of Allium sativum plant (Fig. 4 ).

It has been found that the xylem and phloem have the ability to facilitate the translocation of CQDs in different parts of the plant, which on reaching the leaves begins to accumulate inside the veins. Consequently, the fluorescent marks on the leaf surface demonstrate the accumulation of CQDs in the extracellular spaces of leaf margins. On comparing the data, it has been found that the accumulation rate was maximum in case of P-CQDs treated Allium sativum plant. Furthermore, the uptake of CQDs has been increased linearly with each successive days of treatment (Fig. 4). We have further analysed the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) images of distilled water treated cloves in order to verify the targeted delivery in case of CQDs. Previously researchers have documented the incorporation of carbon dots inside the different parts of plants (Zhang et al., 2018). However, the time-based uptake studies of carbon dots by plants is still a matter of research in literature. From the images, no significant bioluminescence was obtained in case of control treatment. Hence, the obtained CLSM data interprets complete uptake, translocation and accumulation of CQDs in Allium sativum plant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Genotoxic analysis of CQDs on mitotic cell cycle of Allium sativum clove
ce:section.ce:para: The outcomes supported the mutagenic behavior of CQDs at higher concentrations toward Allium sativum root tip cells. The higher concentrations of chosen CQDs have induced the fusion of chromatids with chromosomes and resulted in the formation of chromosomal bridges in Allium sativum root tip cells. In addition, the higher concentrations of CQDs have further restricted the motion of chromosomes to their respective poles and gave rise to the laggard chromosomes. The irreversible deformities in Allium sativum root tip cells appeared in form of sticky chromosomes in presence of higher concentrations of CQDs. These deformities were mainly observed due to the inappropriate association of chromosomes with sub-chromatid bridges (Table S2). In addition, the variations in the chromosomal aberration index was found to be directly dependent upon the concentration and inversely based on the size of the chosen particles. For instance, the maximum reduction in MI was observed for the M-CQDs at 250 ppm, representing the high potential of genotoxicity. Also, the chromosomal aberration index (CAI) values indicate the maximum genotoxicity of M-CQDs at 250 ppm and minimum in case of P-CQDs at 50 ppm. Additionally, the chosen CQDs have provoked a significant reduction in the cell division of meristematic tissue at higher concentration value, which further reduced the mitotic index (M.I) and relative mitotic index (R.M.I) values in Allium sativum root tip cells. The overall reduction in cell division has further been explained due to the higher penetration rate of CQDs which further produced the mito-depressive effect and thus hinders the normal division of cells and give rise to chromosomal aberrations (Fig. 6d).

Recently Chauhan et al. reported that the Allium cepa roots, when exposed to higher concentration of carbon dots results in appearance of chromosomal aberrations due to low absorption potential of roots for such a high concentration and high tendency to disturb mitotic cell cycle (Chauhan et al., 2021b). Many previous experiments have described that the chromosomal aberrations are directly and mitotic index is inversely proportional to concentrations of nanoparticles. Therefore, it has been estimated that the relative lowering in the mitotic index with respect to augmented concentration is probably due to the mitodepressive effect induced by CQDs which further interferes with the normal cell cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total chlorophyll and carotenoid content in CQDs treated Allium sativum leaves
ce:section.ce:para: From the aforementioned results, it has been concluded that P-CQDs induces less genotoxicity and successfully promotes the growth of plant by enhancing the light absorption capacity, chlorophyll metabolism, regulating gene expression and accelerating photosynthetic electron transport. Therefore, the chlorophyll and carotenoids content has been estimated in CQDs treated Allium sativum plant (Table S3). The fresh and green leaves of 45 days old Allium sativum plant were collected from different treatment (control, C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs) and their average chlorophyll and carotenoids content was determined in acetone by using UV–vis. spectrophotometer (Fig. 7 a and b). Interestingly, the plant harvested after 45 days of treatment exhibits fluorescent green colour in leaves under UV illuminator (Fig. 7c). The obtained data has exhibited good agreement with the growth behavior of Allium sativum plant (Fig. 2c). The average chlorophyll content for control, C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs treated Allium sativum plant were 20.60, 22.45, 26.38 and 11.88 mg/L respectively (Fig. 7d). Also, the estimated carotenoids content was found to be 3.27, 2.77, 2.42 and 1.904 for control, C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs treated seedlings (Fig. 7e). In addition, the leaves dispersed in acetone solution emit strong red fluorescence under UV illuminator (Figure S6b). Interestingly, the chlorophyll pigment extracted from P-CQDs and C-CQDs exhibits more fluorescent colour under UV light in comparison to control and M-CQDs. The chlorophyll a content was also analysed with fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum originating from different CQDs treated plants reflects the emission peaks in red region of visible spectrum (Figure S6a). In line to our finding, carbon quantum dots synthesized in previously reported papers, resulted in insignificant amount of chlorophyll content due to lesser number of surface-active functional groups (Milenkovi ć et al., 2021b). Therefore, it has been speculated that enhanced amount of chlorophyll content in P-CQDs was due to more functional moieties over its surface in contrast to C-CQDs and M-CQDs. Hence, the growth enhancement can also be interlinked with the augmented amount of chlorophyll content which depends upon light absorption via photosynthesis process. Table S3 shows the statistical analysis of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b and carotenoids in case of control C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs. In the current study, the outcomes suggest that the chlorophyll amount in leaves were significantly influenced by CQDs treatment. The intensity of colour exhibited by C-CQDs and P-CQDs were comparatively more in contrast to M-CQDs. These outcomes serve as a piece of evidence for the more accumulation and internalization of CQDs by meristematic cells, thereby affecting growth rate of Allium sativum plant (Figs. 2 and 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field cultivation studies of CQDs treated Allium sativum cloves
ce:section.ce:para: Getting the information from cytotoxicity studies of chosen CQDs, it was found that 50 ppm concentration of all the prepared particles were less toxic and further been utilized for the field cultivation studies of Allium sativum. For the analysis, the 7th day old CQDs treated cloves of Allium sativum have been used. The young cloves were sprayed with specific solution of chosen CQDs once a day with well optimized concentration. A stock solution of each treatment i.e. C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs was prepared in 500 ml of deionised water for spraying. The pot with untreated cloves was referred as control. The same volume of deionised water, C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs were sprayed in the respective pots for the comparative studies. The hole present at the bottom of the pot allows the removal of extra water from the soil. The soil testing analysis have shown that the soil does not exhibit any kind of acidic or basic character after treatment with CQDs (Figure S7a). However, the oxidizable organic carbon in soil sample was found to be low to medium range (Figure S7b; Table S4). The average phosphate content of treated soil was found to be 22–56 Kg per hectare (Figure S7c). On the other hand, the ammonical and nitrate nitrogen content values were around 10 and 20 Kg per hectare for the treated soil (Figure S7e and S7f). The length of the shoot was synchronously analysed and recorded subsequently after 10 days of sowing (Fig. 8 ). The analogous values of shoot length were investigated to correlate the corresponding effect of C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs on the growth rate of Allium sativum. The average shoot length was found to be 17, 22 and 34 cm for Allium sativum plants treated with C-CQDs after 10, 20, and 30 days (Fig. 8). Similarly, the shoot length of Allium sativum plants treated with P-CQDs and M-CQDs were found to be 21, 29, 40 cm and 11, 21.5, 31 cm respectively after 10, 20 and 30 days of treatment (Fig. 8). However, shoot length observed in case of control were 20, 27, 38.5 cm. Furthermore, the inadequate growth of Allium sativum plant was witnessed in presence of M-CQDs. The plants have displayed constricted growth up to 10 days of sowing whereas the shoot growth is fastened between 10 and 20 days. The soluble nature of M-CQDs lead to the formation of larger agglomerates of particles which further decrease their intake capacity by plant roots and reduced the growth rate. Therefore, statistically inadequate physiological responses were observed in case of M-CQDs (Fig. 8). However, the cloves treated with P-CQDs particles have manifested the higher growth rate in comparison to the cloves exposed with C-CQDs, M-CQDs and control treatment. A significant enhancement in the shoot length of P-CQDs has further been corroborated with the relatively smaller particle size of P-CQDs. The small size of P-CQDs has further facilitated its easier passage inside the plant cell and enhanced its growth. Further, we have also measured the average fruit weight acquired after 45 days of treatment of Allium sativum plant with different types of CQDs.

The average weight of fruits obtained from control, C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs were found to be 2.45, 2.20, 3.12 and 1.49 g. The outcomes have clearly supported the maximum yield in presence of P-CQDs treated plant (Fig. 8). A comparative view of average shoot and root length of Allium sativum plant after 7- and 45-days treatment with chosen particles have further supported the higher performance of P-CQD followed by C-CQD and M-CQD (Figure S8).

To summarize, the greener, economical and environment friendly approach has been used for the synthesis of CQDs via using the different parts of freshly ripened Aegle marmelos fruit. The work further ascertained the genotoxic and mutagenic effects induced by CQDs on Allium sativum plant. The experimental data further supported the successful translocation of employed CQDs into different parts of Allium sativum plant through vascular system. The CQDs prompted the physiological response in Allium sativum plant and imposed no genotoxicity at 50 ppm concentration. However, the presence of 100 ppm and 250 ppm amount of CQDs has induced the reduction in mitotic index values and enhancement in chromosomal aberrations. Furthermore, it was speculated that the P-CQDs treated cloves have shown the growth enhancement in contrast to other treatments. The obtained results were further verified by fluorescence spectroscopy and confocal laser scanning microscopy. In addition, chlorophyll content of control, C-CQDs, P-CQDs and M-CQDs treated seedlings were found to be 27.134, 26.038, 31.08 and 14.33 mg/L respectively. From all the studies, it was observed that different types of chosen CQDs have displayed significantly different behavior on the root, shoot and chromosomal response of Allium sativum. Furthermore, the concentration dependent responses of Allium sativum seedlings provide new insight to unravel the effects of CQDs. Our investigations unfold the opportunity to utilize Aegle marmelos fruit derived CQDs as a growth regulator in variety of other food plants.
